comments.text: note that if $R(3,n/k)>n$, then $n\le h_3(k)$. thus we should get an optimal construction from the best know bounds for #165. meanwhile, the other bound should not be due to Shearer, since that (I believe) is just about independent sets. instead, you want to quote a result for chromatic number, which I believe is originally due to Johansson. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)

I think this is the other way around - if $R(3,n/k)>n$ then there is a triangle-free graph on $n$ vertices with independence number $\leq n/k$, and so chromatic number $\geq k$, and hence (deleting vertices if necessary to get chromatic number $=k$) $h_3(k) \leq n$. Johansson's bound for the chromatic number of triangle-free graphs (if I've found the right result) says that $\chi(G) \ll \frac{n}{\log n}$, which implies the lower bound $h_3(k)\gg (\log k)k$, which is weaker than the Graver-Yackel bound given here. EDIT: In fact this is the dual function to [1104].

For the record: as in problem 1104, the application of Johansson's theorem is more subtle. It says that $\chi(G)\ll \frac{\Delta}{ \log \Delta}$ where $\Delta$ is the maximum degree of a triangle-free graph $G$. This implies $\chi(G)\ll \sqrt {\frac{n}{\log n}}$, hence $h_3(k) \gg k^2 \log k$. The proof in a nutshell: If $\Delta \leq \sqrt{n \log n}$ then it's immediate. Otherwise select a vertex $v$ with maximum degree and apply induction to $G-N[v]$.
Aha of course, I misread Johansson's result. Incidentally, I can't find a copy of their paper anywhere online, do you know if there's a copy available somewhere?

Johansson's result was in a technical report "Asymptotic choice number for triangle free graphs" from 1996, which he famously never published. There doesn't seem to exist a stable link to the report online, and I have never seen it. However, if I remember well, the write-up in chapters 12 and 13 of the book "Graph colouring and the probabilistic method"(2002) by Michael Molloy and Bruce Reed follows Johansson's proof. It used a rather technical (semi-random) method. Since 2017 there have been shorter proofs and improvements, using e.g. entropy compression, or Lovász Local Lemma, and even just the first moment method.

There is indeed a little mistake in wording here. The correct version should be Denote by $h_3(k)$ the smallest integer for which there is a graph of $h_3(k)$ vertices which contains no triangle and has chromatic number $k$. (note it is not because there is an example on $h_3(k)$ vertices satisfying it, all graphs with higher order have the same property.) The related OEIS sequence is [A292528]. Computing $h_3(k)$ is tough
Thanks for checking Stijn, a crucial couple of words that makes all the difference. Corrected now.
There are triangle free graphs with arbitrarily large chromatic number, so the question as written doesn't seem to make sense.
I don't see why? This question is about the relationship between the number of vertices and chromatic number in triangle-free graphs. The fact you mention is just showing $h_3(k)\to \infty$.

The question implies that for a fixed k, all graphs on sufficiently many vertices with chromatic number k have a triangle. But I can take a triangle free graph with chromatic number k, and then add as many vertices as I want by joining a arbitrarily large path to one of the vertices. This doesn't change the chromatic number or create a triangle.
Hmm, yes. I'll check the source later.
Judging by how it is linked to [920], I'd guess it's meant to be about the largest chromatic number a triangle free graph on $n$ vertices can have.
Note that the latter interpretation was impossible (or way more typos were there); the chromatic number is bounded by the order, so a lower bound above $k^2$ would not be the best
The source was [Er71(-25)] and has been checked.
